name: Paris
attraction: The Eiffel Tower, the 330-meter iron lattice icon built for the 1889 World's Fair.
funFact: Paris has only one stop sign in the entire city — and even that one has been reportedly removed and reinstalled multiple times.

name: Versailles
attraction: The Palace of Versailles, Louis XIV's gilded royal residence with its dazzling Hall of Mirrors.
funFact: The Hall of Mirrors has 357 mirrors, and it's the same room where the treaty ending World War I was signed in 1919.

name: Boulogne-Billancourt
attraction: The Albert Kahn Museum and its themed gardens, ranging from a Japanese village to a Vosges forest.
funFact: The huge Renault factory on Île Seguin shaped the town for the entire 20th century — until it closed in 1992 and is now being turned into a cultural island.

name: Saint-Denis
attraction: The Basilica of Saint-Denis, the world's first Gothic church and royal necropolis of France.
funFact: More than 40 French kings, 30 queens and dozens of princes are buried under one roof here — making it the most royally crowded basement in Europe.

name: Fontainebleau
attraction: The Château de Fontainebleau, a 1,500-room royal palace continuously inhabited by French monarchs for seven centuries.
funFact: Napoleon said his farewell to the Imperial Guard from the château's horseshoe staircase in 1814 — the spot is still called the 'Cour des Adieux'.

name: Lyon
attraction: The Basilica of Notre-Dame de Fourvière, perched on the hill that gives Lyon its panoramic skyline.
funFact: Lyon is the birthplace of cinema — the Lumière brothers shot the very first film, 'Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory', here in 1895.

name: Grenoble
attraction: The Bastille fortress reached by the 'Bulles' — round glass cable-car bubbles that have been climbing the cliff since 1934.
funFact: Grenoble's bubble cable car was one of the very first urban cable cars in the world, and locals nicknamed the spheres 'Les Bulles' (the bubbles).

name: Saint-Étienne
attraction: The Cité du Design, set inside the former state arms factory and now Saint-Étienne's creative landmark.
funFact: Saint-Étienne is France's only UNESCO 'City of Design' — a legacy of its history of weapons, ribbons and bicycles all manufactured here.

name: Annecy
attraction: The Palais de l'Isle, a 12th-century stone prison shaped like a ship's prow in the middle of the Thiou canal.
funFact: Lake Annecy is one of the cleanest lakes in Europe — strict pollution rules adopted in the 1960s mean you can swim almost anywhere along its 35 km of shore.

name: Clermont-Ferrand
attraction: The Cathédrale Notre-Dame-de-l'Assomption, built entirely of jet-black volcanic lava stone.
funFact: The city is the global headquarters of Michelin, and its volcanic-stone cathedral makes it the only major French town that looks like it was carved out of the surrounding extinct volcanoes.

name: Chambéry
attraction: The Fontaine des Éléphants, a quirky monument with four bronze elephants built into a single column.
funFact: Locals nicknamed the elephant fountain 'Les Quatre Sans-Culs' — 'the four bottomless ones' — because only the front halves of the elephants are visible.

name: Valence
attraction: The Kiosque Peynet, the Belle Époque bandstand that inspired the world-famous 'Les Amoureux' (The Lovers) cartoons.
funFact: A 16-year-old Napoleon Bonaparte was stationed in Valence as a young artillery officer — and according to local lore, he had his very first dance lessons here.

name: Lille
attraction: The Vieille Bourse, an ornate 17th-century Flemish stock exchange whose courtyard now hosts a daily second-hand book market.
funFact: Lille was Spanish for almost a century before Louis XIV took it in 1667 — which is why its centre still looks more like Bruges than Paris.

name: Amiens
attraction: The Cathédrale Notre-Dame d'Amiens, the largest Gothic cathedral in France with an interior twice the volume of Paris's Notre-Dame.
funFact: Jules Verne lived in Amiens for 35 years, served on its city council, and is buried in the local Madeleine cemetery — under a sculpture showing him pushing back his own tombstone.

name: Calais
attraction: Rodin's 'Burghers of Calais' bronze, commemorating six citizens who offered their lives to save the besieged town in 1347.
funFact: Calais is closer to England than to Paris — Dover is about 35 km across the Channel, while Paris is nearly 300 km away by road.

name: Roubaix
attraction: La Piscine, an art museum installed inside a stunning 1930s Art Deco municipal swimming pool.
funFact: The pool only stopped welcoming swimmers in 1985 because the roof was on the verge of collapsing — it took 16 years to turn it into the museum it is today.

name: Bordeaux
attraction: The Place de la Bourse with its 'Miroir d'Eau', the world's largest reflecting pool at 3,450 square meters.
funFact: Bordeaux has the largest pedestrianised area of any city in Europe, and its entire 18th-century centre is a single UNESCO World Heritage zone.

name: Limoges
attraction: The Musée National Adrien Dubouché, home to the world's most comprehensive collection of Limoges porcelain.
funFact: Pure white kaolin clay was discovered next to Limoges in 1768 — and within 20 years the city's name had become a worldwide synonym for fine porcelain.

name: Poitiers
attraction: The Romanesque church of Notre-Dame-la-Grande, famous for its richly sculpted 12th-century facade of biblical scenes.
funFact: Poitiers is so historically dense that the University of Poitiers, founded in 1431, briefly counted François Rabelais and René Descartes among its students.

name: La Rochelle
attraction: The three medieval towers (Saint-Nicolas, Chaîne and Lanterne) guarding the entrance to the Old Port.
funFact: When Cardinal Richelieu besieged La Rochelle in 1627, he had a 1.5 km sea wall built across the harbour mouth — the city held out for 14 months and lost 80% of its population.

name: Biarritz
attraction: The Rocher de la Vierge, an offshore rock topped by a Virgin statue and reached by an iron footbridge designed by Eiffel's workshop.
funFact: Biarritz was a sleepy whaling village until Empress Eugénie convinced Napoleon III to build a summer palace here in 1854 — turning it overnight into Europe's most fashionable beach resort.

name: Pau
attraction: The Château de Pau, the Renaissance castle where the future King Henri IV of France was born in 1553.
funFact: Henri IV's cradle was famously a giant turtle shell — and the actual shell is still on display in the castle today.

name: Toulouse
attraction: The Basilique Saint-Sernin, the largest surviving Romanesque church in Europe and a key stop on the Santiago pilgrimage route.
funFact: Nearly every Airbus airliner ever built — including the giant A380 — was assembled in Toulouse, which is why the city's nickname is increasingly 'Aeroville' rather than just 'La Ville Rose'.

name: Montpellier
attraction: The Place de la Comédie, a vast egg-shaped square anchored by the Three Graces fountain and the 19th-century opera house.
funFact: Montpellier's Faculty of Medicine, founded in 1220, is the oldest medical school in the western world still in continuous operation — Rabelais and Nostradamus both studied there.

name: Nîmes
attraction: The Arènes de Nîmes, a Roman amphitheatre from around 70 AD that is still used today for concerts and bullfights.
funFact: The city's coat of arms — a crocodile chained to a palm tree — comes from a Roman coin celebrating the conquest of Egypt, and Hermès later borrowed the motif for one of its silk scarves.

name: Perpignan
attraction: The Palace of the Kings of Majorca, a fortified 13th-century citadel from when Perpignan was capital of the short-lived Majorcan kingdom.
funFact: Salvador Dalí once declared Perpignan's train station to be 'the centre of the universe' after a mystical vision — and even painted a canvas of it in 1965.

name: Carcassonne
attraction: The Cité de Carcassonne, a fully fortified medieval town with double ramparts and 52 towers.
funFact: Carcassonne was about to be demolished in the 1850s when architect Viollet-le-Duc convinced the state to restore it instead — and a German board game named after it has now sold over 12 million copies.

name: Albi
attraction: The Cathédrale Sainte-Cécile, the largest brick cathedral in the world, built like a fortress after the Cathar wars.
funFact: Painter Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec was born in Albi in 1864, and the city's museum holds the largest collection of his works anywhere in the world.

name: Strasbourg
attraction: Strasbourg Cathedral, whose single 142-meter pink sandstone spire was the tallest building in the world from 1647 to 1874.
funFact: Every day at 12:30, the cathedral's astronomical clock springs to life and a tiny procession of the apostles bows before a mechanical Christ — drawing crowds for over 175 years.

name: Reims
attraction: The Cathédrale Notre-Dame de Reims, the Gothic coronation church where 33 French kings were crowned.
funFact: Beneath Reims runs more than 250 km of chalk tunnels, originally Roman quarries, now used by champagne houses to age millions of bottles at a constant 10°C.

name: Metz
attraction: The Centre Pompidou-Metz, a futuristic art museum topped by a vast woven hexagonal roof inspired by a Chinese hat.
funFact: Metz's train station, built by the Germans in 1908 during the annexation, has been voted 'most beautiful train station in France' multiple times in national polls.

name: Nancy
attraction: Place Stanislas, an 18th-century gilded-gate masterpiece widely considered one of the most beautiful royal squares in Europe.
funFact: The square was built by an exiled king of Poland, Stanisław Leszczyński, who became Duke of Lorraine and decided to spend his retirement turning Nancy into a tiny Versailles.

name: Colmar
attraction: The 'Little Venice' quarter, a postcard row of half-timbered houses lining the Lauch river canal.
funFact: Colmar is the hometown of Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi — the sculptor who designed the Statue of Liberty — and the city even has a 12-meter replica of her on its northern roundabout.

name: Troyes
attraction: The medieval old town shaped like a champagne cork, packed with timber-framed houses along the narrow Ruelle des Chats.
funFact: The unit of measurement called 'troy ounce', still used today for gold and silver, takes its name from the great medieval trade fairs held in Troyes.

name: Marseille
attraction: The Basilica of Notre-Dame de la Garde, the 'Bonne Mère' standing 162 meters above the city on its limestone outcrop.
funFact: Marseille is the oldest city in France, founded around 600 BC by Greek sailors from Phocaea — meaning it's about 800 years older than Paris.

name: Nice
attraction: The Promenade des Anglais, a 7-kilometer seafront walkway lined with palm trees and Belle Époque hotels.
funFact: The Promenade was paid for in the 1820s by the city's wintering English aristocrats — which is why a French seafront in Italy-flavoured Nice has an English name.

name: Toulon
attraction: Mont Faron, a 584-meter clifftop reached by a small cable car offering a panoramic view of France's largest naval base.
funFact: Napoleon Bonaparte first made his name as a 24-year-old artillery officer at the 1793 siege of Toulon — and got himself promoted from captain to brigadier general in just three months.

name: Aix-en-Provence
attraction: The Cours Mirabeau, a plane-tree-shaded avenue lined with Baroque mansions, fountains and historic cafés.
funFact: Aix is nicknamed the 'city of a thousand fountains' — there are well over a hundred and several of them spout naturally warm water from underground thermal springs.

name: Avignon
attraction: The Palais des Papes, the largest Gothic palace in the world and seat of seven popes during the 14th century.
funFact: For 68 years, popes ruled the Catholic Church not from Rome but from Avignon — and the city stayed officially papal property until the French Revolution confiscated it in 1791.

name: Cannes
attraction: The Palais des Festivals et des Congrès, home of the Cannes Film Festival and the famous red-carpeted steps.
funFact: The Palme d'Or trophy is made of 118 grams of 24-carat gold mounted on a rock-crystal cushion — and the design is updated each year by Chopard, the official jeweller.

name: Nantes
attraction: Les Machines de l'Île, a steampunk wonderland centred on a 12-meter mechanical elephant that walks the old shipyards.
funFact: Nantes's giant mechanical elephant carries up to 50 passengers on its back, sprays water from its trunk, and was inspired by the imaginative writings of native son Jules Verne.

name: Angers
attraction: The Château d'Angers, a striped fortress of slate and limestone hosting the 14th-century Apocalypse Tapestry.
funFact: The Apocalypse Tapestry is the largest medieval tapestry ensemble ever woven — over 100 meters long — and was once cut up into rugs and horse blankets before being rescued in the 19th century.

name: Le Mans
attraction: The Cité Plantagenêt, a remarkably preserved medieval old town encircled by 1,300 meters of Gallo-Roman walls.
funFact: The 24 Hours of Le Mans is so demanding that the winning car typically covers more than 5,000 km — roughly the distance from Paris to New York — in a single race.

name: Rouen
attraction: Rouen Cathedral, the Gothic giant Monet famously painted in over 30 light-and-time-of-day variations.
funFact: Joan of Arc was burned at the stake in Rouen's Place du Vieux-Marché in 1431 — and a striking modern church now stands on the exact spot, with a roof shaped like flames.

name: Caen
attraction: The Château de Caen, one of the largest medieval fortresses in Europe, founded around 1060 by William the Conqueror.
funFact: About 70% of Caen was destroyed in the summer of 1944 during the Battle of Normandy — yet William the Conqueror's 11th-century stone keep and abbeys still stand among the rebuilt streets.

name: Le Havre
attraction: The reconstructed city centre by Auguste Perret, a striking 1950s ensemble of pre-cast concrete and the soaring Saint-Joseph church.
funFact: Le Havre is the only 20th-century European city centre listed by UNESCO as World Heritage — recognition that a town rebuilt entirely in concrete after WWII could still be a masterpiece.

name: Honfleur
attraction: The Vieux Bassin, a picture-perfect old harbour ringed by tall slate-fronted houses that inspired Boudin and the Impressionists.
funFact: Honfleur's church of Sainte-Catherine was built entirely of wood by local shipbuilders after the Hundred Years War — its vaulted ceiling looks remarkably like an upside-down hull.

name: Rennes
attraction: The Parlement de Bretagne, a 17th-century palace whose interior was nearly destroyed in a spectacular 1994 fire and meticulously rebuilt.
funFact: Half of Rennes burned to the ground in 1720 in a six-day fire reportedly started by a drunk carpenter — which is why much of the centre is granite and slate rather than the original timber.

name: Brest
attraction: The Château de Brest, France's oldest continuously occupied castle and home today to the National Maritime Museum.
funFact: Brest was so heavily bombed during World War II that around 90% of the city was flattened — yet the medieval castle survived almost untouched and is still used by the French Navy.

name: Quimper
attraction: The twin-spired Cathédrale Saint-Corentin, which is famously slightly bent — its nave doesn't quite line up with its choir.
funFact: Quimper has been producing its hand-painted faïence pottery non-stop since 1690, making it one of the longest-running ceramic traditions in France.

name: Saint-Malo
attraction: The granite ramparts encircling the walled corsair city, offering a 1.7-kilometer wraparound view of the Channel.
funFact: From 1590 to 1594 Saint-Malo declared itself an independent republic, and locals still proudly say 'Ni Français, ni Breton, Malouin suis' — neither French nor Breton, but Malouin.

name: Vannes
attraction: The medieval ramparts and the polychrome timber-framed houses lining Place Henri IV.
funFact: On a corner house in the old town, two grinning carved granite heads called 'Vannes et sa Femme' (Vannes and his Wife) have been smiling at passers-by since the 16th century.

name: Dijon
attraction: The Palais des Ducs et des États de Bourgogne, the former seat of the powerful Dukes of Burgundy.
funFact: Dijon mustard is a culinary style, not a protected origin — almost none of the mustard sold under the name is actually produced in or near Dijon today.

name: Besançon
attraction: The Citadelle de Besançon, a 17th-century fortress by Vauban perched 100 meters above a horseshoe bend in the Doubs river.
funFact: Besançon is the birthplace of Victor Hugo and of the Lumière brothers, and was for nearly two centuries the watchmaking capital of France.

name: Beaune
attraction: The Hospices de Beaune, a 1443 charity hospital famed for its multicoloured glazed-tile Burgundian roof.
funFact: The Hospices still own vineyards donated by patients over the centuries, and their annual wine auction every November sets reference prices for fine Burgundy worldwide.

name: Tours
attraction: The Cathédrale Saint-Gatien, a Gothic gem with 13th-century stained glass and a Renaissance facade.
funFact: Linguists have long held that the French spoken around Tours has the most neutral accent in the country — which is why dubbing studios in Paris regularly recruit voice actors from the area.

name: Orléans
attraction: The Cathédrale Sainte-Croix, whose stained-glass windows tell the story of Joan of Arc lifting the siege of Orléans in 1429.
funFact: Every year on May 8, Orléans throws a costumed parade to celebrate Joan of Arc — a tradition that has been running, with very few interruptions, since 1430.

name: Bourges
attraction: The Cathédrale Saint-Étienne de Bourges, a UNESCO Gothic masterpiece famous for being built without a transept.
funFact: By skipping the usual cross-shaped transept, the architects of Bourges Cathedral created an enormous unobstructed nave that still feels jaw-droppingly tall and open eight centuries later.

name: Ajaccio
attraction: The Maison Bonaparte, the modest townhouse where Napoleon was born in 1769 and now a national museum.
funFact: When Napoleon was born, Corsica had been French for barely 15 months — meaning the most famous Frenchman in history was technically born just in time to be French.

name: Bastia
attraction: The Vieux Port and the towering 18th-century Église Saint-Jean-Baptiste that overlooks it.
funFact: Bastia served as the capital of Corsica for over 300 years — and was only stripped of the title in 1791, when revolutionary France handed it to Napoleon's hometown of Ajaccio instead.
